coredata.dc:title: N, S/P co-doped hemicellulose-based carbon dots with tunable fluorescence for anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Qichao
Guo, Xiaohui
Meng, Ling
Liu, Mengru
Chen, Li
Li, Hailong
Hu, Jian
coredata.dc:description: Based on the status quo of high energy consumption and low utilization of nonfibrous components in traditional pulp and paper industry, a sustainable and facile approach was proceeded to realize the high-value utilization of hemicelluloses from papermaking waste liquor. The hemicellulose waste produced by ethanol precipitation in pre-hydrolysis liquor (PHL), was directly used to fabricate carbon dots (CDs) via a hydrothermal method. The hydrothermal carbonization and heteroatoms doping contributed to the sp2 conjugated domains and surface defect states of CDs, thus creating the bright blue (N-CDs), deep cyan (N/S-CDs), and light cyan (N/P-CDs) fluorescence under UV radiation. The XPS analysis and density functional theory (DFT) calculations demonstrated that the large sp2 conjugated system and the synergistic effect of CO, N-(C)3, CS, and PO groups promoted the narrow of band gap and the red-shift of fluorescence emission. Importantly, the prepared CDs grew in situ on cotton fibers, showed excellent fluorescent performance. The obtained CDs could be also utilized to prepare anti-counterfeiting film and ink due to their excellent optical features, verifying the great potential application as security material. The feasible strategy of the high-value conversion of biomass waste opens a window of opportunity for the practical anti-counterfeiting utilizations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hemicellulose waste
Heteroatom-doped carbon dots
High-value utilization
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting film and ink

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation of Guangdong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation ( 2019B151502043 ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22208069 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of hemicellulose-based CDs with multiple elemental doping for anti-counterfeiting application.
TEM, HRTEM images (top inset) and particle size distribution histograms (down inset) of (a) N-CDs, (b) N/S-CDs and (c) N/P-CDs under their optimal synthesis conditions.
PL emission spectra with 0.1 mg·mL−1 concentration at 5 nm slit width and fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs (a, d), N/S-CDs (b, e) and N/P-CDs (c, f). UV–vis spectrums (g) and Tauc plots (h) of CDs.
(a) FT-IR, (b) XRD, (c) XPS survey spectrum, (d-f) C1s, (g-i) N1s and (j-l) O1s of N-CDs, N/S-CDs and N/P-CDs. (m) S2p of N/S-CDs, (n) P2p of N/P-CDs.
The structure models, molecular orbital diagrams, energy levels and respective bandgaps of chemical bonds in CDs at the B3LYP/6-31g (d, p) level.
Schematic diagram of the fluorescence mechanism of N-CDs, N/S-CDs and N/P-CDs.

The fluorescence intensity changes of (a) N-CDs (b) N/S-CDs (c) N/P-CDs in solutions with different pH values, and (d) fluorescence intensity variation (F/F0) of CDs on the concentration of KCl solutions (0–1000 mM).

(a) Photographs of fluorescent films with (i) N-CDs, (ii) N/S-CDs and (iii) N/P-CDs, respectively, and anti-counterfeiting graphics with (ii) N-CDs, (iii) N/S-CDs and (iv) N/P-CDs on (b) polypropylene paper, (c) cellulose-based filter paper and (d) nonwoven substrate.
The images of (a) N-CDs/cotton paper, (b) N/S-CDs film, and (c) printed graphic based N/P-CDs at 365 nm excitation, (i) before and (ii) after 2 weeks light bleaching test.
Main chemical constituents of the PHL.

head.ce:title: N, S/P co-doped hemicellulose-based carbon dots with tunable fluorescence for anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Light Industry and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, CN 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Light Industry and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Light Industry and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, CN 510640, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Huangpu Hydrogen Energy Innovation Center, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Huangpu Hydrogen Energy Innovation Center, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou, Guangdong, 510006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Based on the status quo of high energy consumption and low utilization of nonfibrous components in traditional pulp and paper industry, a sustainable and facile approach was proceeded to realize the high-value utilization of hemicelluloses from papermaking waste liquor. The hemicellulose waste produced by ethanol precipitation in pre-hydrolysis liquor (PHL), was directly used to fabricate carbon dots (CDs) via a hydrothermal method. The hydrothermal carbonization and heteroatoms doping contributed to the sp2 conjugated domains and surface defect states of CDs, thus creating the bright blue (N-CDs), deep cyan (N/S-CDs), and light cyan (N/P-CDs) fluorescence under UV radiation. The XPS analysis and density functional theory (DFT) calculations demonstrated that the large sp2 conjugated system and the synergistic effect of CO, N-(C)3, CS, and PO groups promoted the narrow of band gap and the red-shift of fluorescence emission. Importantly, the prepared CDs grew in situ on cotton fibers, showed excellent fluorescent performance. The obtained CDs could be also utilized to prepare anti-counterfeiting film and ink due to their excellent optical features, verifying the great potential application as security material. The feasible strategy of the high-value conversion of biomass waste opens a window of opportunity for the practical anti-counterfeiting utilizations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that support our findings are available from the corresponding author in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Pulp and papermaking, a traditional pillar industry of national economy, focus on the production of pulp and paper in the large-scale industrialization, which realized the effective utilization of cellulose component. In traditional pulping process, the presence of non-cellulosic components (hemicellulose and lignin) resulted in the products quality reduction, increasement of chemical consumption. Statistically, about 50 % of hemicellulose component was dissolved in waste liquor and applied to power generation with low heating value (13.6 MJ/kg), causing huge waste of energy [1]. The combination of pre-hydrolysis and subsequent pulping has been regarded as a cost-effectiveness method for the effective separation of cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin components and dissolved pulp production in view of biorefinery [2,3]. Pre-hydrolysis could lead to the removal of large amount of hemicellulose and minor lignin prior to pulping, and the waste hemicellulose derivatives can be further converted to high-value added products, such as, chemicals, xylitol, and fuel ethanol, realizing the high-value utilization [1]. It is beneficial for the sustainable development of pulp and papermaking industry, and help to reduce the pressure from environment pollution and energy shortage.

As a new ‘zero-dimensional’ carbon nanomaterial in 2004 [4], carbon dots (CDs) have been considered as an ideal substitute for semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes because of their outstanding fluorescence, optical stability, easy surface modification, excellent biocompatibility, water-solubility and low toxicity [5,6]. Up to now, CDs have exhibited broad applications prospects in multiple domains including the biological imaging [7], fluorescent anti-counterfeiting [8,9], detection and sensing [10], optoelectronic devices [11], and disease treatment [12], etc.

Given the advantages and variability of CDs in the application fields, the preparations of CDs have been studied widely. In particular, renewable and green biomass-based carbon materials have been strongly developed, in response to the increasing environmental crisis and serious decline of fossil energy. Especially, ‘green’ CDs are prepared from the renewable biomass with high carbon content, due to the exhibited attractive social and economic value based on the low cost, wide distribution, easy synthesis and excellent biocompatibility [6]. Until now, tremendous efforts have been conducted to convert pure biomass or biomass wastes into biomass-based CDs. Cellulose- or starch-based CDs had been produced for the detection of Hg+ [13] or bioimaging and other biological domains [14]. The biomass wastes, like cheap and green eggs, rose flowers and corn residue were used as precursor and synthesized into the fluorescent CDs for optoelectronic fields [15], tetracycline sensing [16], and bioimaging and detection [17], respectively. Expanding the carbon source types has become a hotspot and attracts much attention of researchers. It is known that the synthetic technique of CDs is closely related with the energy consume and production cost, directly influencing the large-scale production and application of CDs. To solve this, hydrothermal treatment was chosen in this work. It's considered as a facile, low cost and effective technology, compared with the traditional methods, such as plasma pyrolysis, microwave treatment, electro-chemical oxidation and thermal oxidation [18].

Commonly, to improve the fluorescence stability and enrich the functionality of CDs, CDs were doped by heteroatoms (e.g. N, S and P) to regulate their electronic structure and change the photoelectronic transfer gap between their lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) [19]. In this way, their photoluminescence properties are regulated and more functionalization could be fully demonstrated. Specifically, the association of heteroatoms (e.g. N, S and P) could modify the carbon-skeleton structure with different specialties. Also, the co-existence of various heteroatoms can improve diverse performances due to the synergetic effect of different chemical moieties [20]. Moreover, it is a widespread assumption that the photoluminescence (PL) emission of CDs derives from the common effect of the core state and surface defect states, while the multiple doping of heteroatoms endows CDs with nonstoichiometric structural features, making the tailor-control exploration complicated. The PL emission mechanism had been under discussion, including quantum confinement effect [21], different chemical compositions of CDs [22], interaction between CDs particles [23] and multiple emission centers [24]. Clarifying the relationship between PL emission properties and chemical structures is extremely challenging for tuning the PL emission of target CDs. It is hypothesized that the separated biomass waste-hemicellulose in pre-hydrolysis liquor (PHL) could be used as a high-quality carbon source for synthesizing the tunable fluorescent biomass-based CDs. Then, we further attempted to prepare fluorescent fibers, film and ink, endowing them with excellent fluorescence characteristics, which could promote the application of hemicellulose in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting, thus start a new avenue for the high-value conversion of biomass waste.

Firstly, the hemicellulose hydrolysate (mainly xylan) in PHL were separated by alcohol precipitation and used to prepare CDs via hydrothermal method, followed by N, N/S, or N/P doping. The average size of X-CDs (X = N, N/S, N/P) ranges from 3.8 to 5.0 nm, possessing great water solubility, photoluminescence, pH and saline stability. To clarifying the PL emission mechanism, the fluorescent properties and chemical structures of X-CDs were explored by PL spectra, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) and density functional theory (DFT) calculation characterizations. Then, the CDs/cotton fibers with great fluorescent performance were prepared by an in situ hydrothermal method. Importantly, the CDs with excellent optical characteristics were used to prepare anti-counterfeiting film and ink as shown in Fig. 1 . This strategy presents a novel approach for the high-value conversion of hemicellulose waste and is significant for promoting the application in anti-counterfeiting field.

Poplar chips were obtained from Sun Paper Co., Ltd. First, the poplar chips were manually screened and washed, then dried and placed in a polyethylene self-sealing bag to balance moisture for later use. Anhydrous ethanol, sulfuric acid, sodium hydroxide and potassium chloride were purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group (Shanghai, China). Tris (2-aminoethyl) amine, ammonium thiocyanate and diamine hydrogen phosphate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and act as N, N/S and N/P dopants, respectively. Polyvinyl alcohol 124 (PVA 124) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent CO., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Organic silicon defoamer was purchased from Macklin Biochemical CO., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) and urea were produced from FUCHEN Chemical Reagent CO., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The cotton pulp was from a cotton pulp factory in China.

PHL was prepared according to the previous research of Li et al. [25]. A horizontal digester (No. 2611, KRK company, Tokyo, Japan) with temperature control was used to pretreat poplar wood chips. The pretreatment conditions were shown as follows: the ratio of solid to liquid was 1:6 (w/v), the maximum temperature was 170 °C, the heating and holding time were 60 min with the heating rate about 2.5 °C/min. After the reaction, the digester was cooled down to room temperature with cool water. Afterwards, the PHL was filtrated with a 200-mesh filter cloth and then stored at refrigerator for later analysis or use.

The extraction of the dissolved hemicelluloses in PHL was completed by ethanol precipitation [26]. A certain amount of anhydrous ethanol was added (volume ratio of ethanol/PHL = 5:1), followed by stirring for 10 min and leaving to set for 8 h. The mixture was centrifuged, and the sediments (represented by xylan in our experiments) were separated and washed to neutral with deionized water. Then, the obtained xylan was freeze-dried.

0.1 g obtained xylan mixed with a certain of tris (2-aminoethyl) amine (TAEA), ammonium thiocyanate, or diamine hydrogen phosphate were dissolved in 40 mL deionized water, ultrasound-treated for 10 min, and stirred for 20 min at 500 rpm. The mixture was transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor. Then, the hydrothermal treatment proceeded at high temperature (160, 180, 200 °C) for a certain time (6, 8, 10 h). After the reaction, the reactor was naturally cooled to room temperature, the suspension was filtered via 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove large impurities, and then dialyzed for 24 h using a 500 Da dialysis membrane. Finally, the obtained X-CDs were freeze-dried and named as N-CDs, N/S-CDs and N/P-CDs, respectively.

Alkali pretreatment of cotton fibers was carried out for removing wax, grease and other non-cellulose components. 0.4 g cotton fibers were immersed in 40 mL of 10 % NaOH solution, and exposed to ultrasound treatment for 20 min. The mixture was placed to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor at 160 °C for 4 h. Subsequently, the cotton fibers were washed with ethanol and water in turn to neutral and dried at 40 °C in an oven.

The fluorescent CDs/cotton fibers were prepared by in-situ hydrothermal method. 0.3 g pretreated cotton fibers were mixed to the reaction system in “Section 2.4. Preparation of CDs”, and the hydrothermal reaction was conducted under the set conditions. After the completion of synthesis, the obtained cotton fibers were filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane, then transferred to ultrasonic bath in deionized water for 10 min, washed three times with ethanol and water in turn. Finally, the fibers were dried in an oven at 40 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting film and ink
ce:section.ce:para: 5 g PVA was dissolved in 100 mL deionized water under light stirring (400 rpm) at 95 °C for 2 h until the homogeneous and transparent 5 wt% PVA solution was prepared. The cooled PVA solution was then mixed with the prepared CDs using a stirrer at room temperature for 1 h until homogeneity. The CDs-PVA solutions were dropped into the polyvinyl culture dish and dried at 40 °C for 48 h to obtain the transparent CDs-PVA film.

The fluorescent CDs-PVA ink were produced as follows. 0.5 wt% organic silicone defoamer, 1 wt% SDS, 1 wt% urea and 1 wt% CDs was added to the above PVA solution using a water bath with a magnetic stirrer at 40 °C for 1 h. Then, the CDs-PVA ink were prepared for printable anti-counterfeiting.

The sugar components of PHL were determined using ion chromatography (IC, Thermo Fisher Scientific Dionex ICS-5000 DC, USA) equipped with a CarboPac PA10 column. The degradation components were detected using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, SGLC LC-20CE, Japan) with a Diamonsil C18 (2) column. The content of lignin was detected using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent UV-2600i, USA) at 205 nm. PL emission spectra, fluorescence decay curves and quantum yield (QY) of CDs were recorded on a fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA FLUOROMAX-4, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8, Germany) equipped with a Cu/Ka radiation tube was applied to record the X-ray spectra of CDs at 40 kV/40 mA. Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, VERTEX-70, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS-ULTRA DLD, UK) were applied to analyze the chemical functional groups and structures of CDs. UV–vis (Agilent UV-2600i, USA) was employed for detecting the UV absorption properties of CDs. The morphology of CDs was observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM 2100, Japan) with an ultra-thin carbon mesh for placing samples. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was used to determine the lattice spacing of CDs. For DFT calculations, the frequency optimization was performed at the B3LYP with the 6-31G (d) basis set. The Gaussian 09 program and Gauss view 5 software were used to operate all the calculations and generate the molecular orbitals, respectively. The fluorescence microscope images of CDs/cotton fibers were obtained by using a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Leica TCS-SP5, Germany).

During the pre-hydrolysis, H3O+ produced by water autoionization and acetic acid originated from the cleavage of acetyl in hemicellulose at high temperature broke the glycosidic bonds of hemicellulose and promoted the dissolution of each component into the PHL [27]. From Table 1 , the main chemical compositions of PHL included saccharides (monomers and oligomers), lignin, and a small number of degraded by-products, i.e., acetic acid, furfural, 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (5-HMF). Saccharides were the main hydrolysate of hemicellulose with the total concentration of 14.87 g/L. Xylan and xylose occupied the large proportion of 63.55 % and 11.77 %, and their concentrations were 9.45 g/L and 1.75 g/L, respectively. The depolymerization of lignin also occurred with the content of 5.25 g/L. Acetic acid was 1.94 g/L, which generated from the splitting of the acetyl group in the hemicellulose side chain. In addition, a small quantity of furfural (0.75 g/L) and 5-HMF (0.08 g/L) were derived from the degradation of pentose and hexose.
In this work, the synthetic conditions of X-CDs were analyzed, including the mass ratio of reactants, heating temperature and duration. A 3*3 orthogonal experiment was designed in Table S1.

Fig. S1 showed the PL emission spectra of CDs for selecting the optimal hydrothermal conditions. From Fig. S1 and Table S2, the optimal reaction conditions of N-CDs and N/S-CDs were A1B3C3 and that of N/P-CDs was A1B1C2. The PL intensity of N/P-CDs (A1B1C2) was much higher than that of A1B2C2 after screening. The PL intensity of CDs was arranged as follows: N-CDs > N/S-CDs > N/P-CDs. And their corresponding production yield was shown in Table S3. It could be preliminarily considered that not only the species of heteroatoms, but also the interactions between them may affect the PL emission behaviors of CDs due to the introduction of new surface chemical groups.

TEM was used to investigate the morphological structure of CDs. Fig. 2a-c provided the TEM, HRTEM and particle size distribution of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2, all the CDs had a uniform spherical nanostructure, and the average particle size of N-CDs, N/S-CDs and N/P-CDs was 3.8 mm, 4.4 mm and 5.0 mm, respectively, indicating their good dispersibility in water without agglomeration. The HRTEM images indicated (top-right insets of Fig. 2) a clear lattice fringes of 0.22 nm, which was corresponded to the (100) crystal plane of sp2 graphite carbon [25]. The results illustrated that all of CDs had a graphene-like crystalline structure. The discrepancy in particle size of the three CDs corresponded to the different physical structures, which may be attributed to the difference of reaction conditions, including the mass ratio of xylan and dopants, heating temperature and time, as shown in “Section 3.2. The optimum fabrication of CDs”.

The PL emission spectra of CDs (0.1 mg·mL−1) were shown in Fig. 3a-c, which exhibited colorless transparent under sunlight and bright blue and light blue fluorescence, respectively, under the 365 nm excitation (top-right inset of Fig. 3). However, in Fig. S2, the CDs solutions at high concentration were bright bule, deep cyan and light cyan, respectively, due to the different size distribution and surface emissive sites of CDs [29,30]. All the three CDs possessed excitation-dependent behavior. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of N-CDs (QY = 8.1 %) were 350 nm and 450 nm, respectively (Fig. 3a), that of N/S-CDs (QY = 3.7 %) were 360 nm and 455 nm, respectively, which were red-shifted in comparison with that of N-CDs. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of N/P-CDs (QY = 2.9 %) were blue-shifted to 320 nm and 415 nm, respectively, while N/P-CDs emitted the light blue fluorescence, different from N-CDs, at 365 nm excitation. This phenomenon showed the differing excitation dependent emission performances of X-CDs. The discrepant fluorescence of CDs may be induced by multiple factors such as quantum confined effect [31], surface defects [32]. The conjugated domains were the main origin of CDs fluorescence, the size of which may be the key to enhancing CDs fluorescence. In addition, the doping of heteroatoms (such as N, S or P) contributes to forming abundant functional groups at the edges, while the substituents will also affect the extent of delocalization of π-electron systems of CDs, further alter the electronic states and emission of CDs. The decay curves of CDs were exhibited in Fig. 3d-f, the fitted biexponential decay curves of all the three CDs had two components, the fast one (τ1) was closely related to the radiative recombination of excitons in the core structure, the surface states were stimulated to produce a slow fluorescence lifetime (τ2) due to the non-radiation recombination of photoexcited carriers on the surface. The average τ of CDs was 3.3, 2.5, and 2.3 ns, respectively. This revealed that the fluorescence properties of CDs with different heteroatom doping were certainly controlled by both their sp2 conjugated system and surface functional groups. The complicated surface functional groups can be divided into two types, i.e., electronic-donating and electronic-withdrawing substituents, which endow the different excited energy levels in the CDs structure, and further alter the fluorescence of CDs. Thus, the clear interrelation of the groups and sp2 conjugated system should be explored to improve and modulate CDs PL emission.

UV–vis spectra of CDs were depicted in Fig. 3g. The three CDs had a high absorption peak at around 270 nm, due to the π-π* transition of the CC sp2 conjugated domains in the core of CDs [10,33,34]. In addition, the weak absorption peak at 325 nm generated from the n-π* electron transition related to CO and N-/S-/P- containing bonds of CDs [32]. Their absorption intensities are related to the conjugated structure in the carbon core and some surface defects states, respectively. Thus, the UV–vis absorption performances of CDs can be transferred by diverse heteroatom doping. In order to determine the optical band gap (Eg) of CDs, the UV–vis spectra of X-CDs were converted to Tauc plots ((αhν)2 versus hν), where α, h and ν stand for absorption coefficient, Planck's constant and optical frequency, respectively. The Eg is the intersection value on the X-axis. As described in Fig. 3h, the Eg of N-CDs, N/S-CDs and N/P-CDs were 5.58, 5.34 and 5.58 eV, respectively. The physicochemical structure of CDs was closely related to their energy levels and PL emission behaviors.

FT-IR spectra exhibited the typical functional groups of xylan and three X-CDs (Fig. 4a). The peak located at 3425 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of OH. The stretching vibration peak at 2925 cm−1 was assigned to the CH from sp3 carbon [35,36]. The peak occurred at 1732 cm−1 was the CO derived from stretching vibrations of the acetyl group in xylan [37]. While the peak at 1616 cm−1 was caused by the vibration of the benzene ring structure due to the presence of lignin in xylan. Besides, the vibration absorption band of NH at about 3220 cm−1 [38], the absorption peak of C-O-H/C-O-C bond at 1035 cm−1, and the absorption peak of CO (1630 cm−1) and CN (1375 cm−1) demonstrated that all the three CDs possessed O-related and N-related functional groups [39]. The absorption peaks of N/S-CDs and N/P-CDs located at 2066 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1 corresponded to the SH and PO bonds, respectively, implying the successful doping of S and P.

The XRD patterns (Fig. 4b) showed the crystal structures of xylan and CDs, all of which had a wide diffraction peak. The diffraction peak of xylan at approximately 19.8° corresponded to the (110) crystal plane of hemicellulose, while the broad peak at around 22.4° of the three CDs corresponded to the graphitic interlayer stacking (002) crystal plane [31]. The FT-IR and XRD results of the CDs indicated that the CDs might be a graphite-like carbon skeleton structure with abundant oxygen-containing groups, some N-related groups, and a small amount of S/P-related groups for N/S- or N/P-CDs.

To figure out the effects of different dopants (N, S and P), the XPS results of CDs were shown in Fig. 4c-n and Table S4. XPS survey spectra of all CDs showed three obvious characteristic peaks at 283.9 eV, 399.9 eV and 531.9 eV, which assigned to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The weak peak at 163.9 and 134.8 eV corresponded to S2p and P2p, respectively. These implied the successful doping of N, S and P. In Fig. 4d-f, the peak fitting of C1s showed three main peaks at 284.7 (C=C/C-C), 285.3 (C-O/C-N for N-CDs, C-O/C-S for N/S-CDs) or 286.3 (C-P/C-N), and 288.8 eV (C=O), respectively. From Fig. 4g-i, the N1s XPS spectra of N-CDs and N/S-CDs were fitted to three peaks, including C-N-C (399.4 eV for N-CDs, 399.5 eV for N/S-CDs), N-(C)3 (399.7 eV for N-CDs，400.2 eV for N/S-CDs) and NH (400.6 eV for N-CDs, 401.4 eV for N/S-CDs). The N1s of N/P-CDs showed three peaks located at 399.2 (C-N-C), 400.0 (NH) and 400.9 eV (N-N/P-N). The O1s of XPS results were exhibited in Fig. 4g-l, the peak of CO and CO appeared in three CDs, while the signal at 533.5 eV implied the presence of PO in N/P-CDs. The S2p spectrum of N/S-CDs was fitted into three peaks at 163.9, 165.4 and 169.6 eV, attributed to C-S-S2p3/2, C-S-S2p1/2 and CS, respectively (Fig. 4m). Fig. 4n showed the P2p peak of N/P-CDs, the peak at 133.6 eV and 134.0 eV belonged to P-C/P-N and PO group, respectively. The detailed contents of the chemical bonds of CDs were also recorded in Fig. 4. The incremental sp2 C content of CDs indicated the gradual increased sp2 conjugated structure from N-, N/S- to N/P-CDs, which was consistent with the analyzed result of TEM. The decay curves and XPS results confirmed that the PL emission characteristic of CDs derived from the combined contribution of the intrinsic states and surface defect states. At the specific excitation wavelength, emission defects emitted photons with different energy in the radiation recombination process, which may attribute to the excitation dependent emission behavior of CDs. The discrepant contents of N-, P-, O- and S-related groups contributed to introducing various surface defects, generated by the molecular hybridization of carbon skeleton and the linked chemical functional groups [35], which partly respond to their different PL emission properties.

To further explore the fluorescence mechanism of X-CDs, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels and band gaps were obtained by DFT calculation. From Fig. 5 , the band gaps decreased from 4.03 to 1.62 eV with the number of benzene rings increasing from 7 to 14, indicating that the larger sp2 conjugated domains resulted in the narrow of band gaps and the red-shift of PL emission [34]. The emission properties of the obtained CDs could be partly controlled by the size-dependent effect. CDs with 7 benzene rings and different O-, N-, S- and P-related groups were also simulated to explore the influence of surface groups on the energy levels. Due to the addition of C-N-C and NH2 groups, the band gap slightly decreased to 3.92 eV and 3.97 eV, respectively. While the band gap decreased to 2.35 eV with the addition of N-(C)3, indicating that N doped in the form of N-(C)3 conducted to the reduction of band gap. When the CO, CS or PO group was added, the band gap decreased to 0.48, 1.71 and 3.89 eV, respectively. The results proved that both a large sp2 conjugated system and abundant O- or S-related groups (CO, CS) can significantly reduce the band gap and induce the red-shift of PL emission, which was similar to the previous theoretical calculations [40]. In order to analyze the synergistic and antagonistic effect of multiple substituents for the modulation of PL emission, the models with 7 benzene rings and single-doped or co-doped groups were also listed. The co-existence of C-N-C and NH2 changed the band gap to 3.90 eV without marked diminution compared to single doping of C-N-C or NH2, implying that C-N-C and NH2 group was not the main factor for the long wavelength PL emission. While, the addition of N-(C)3, CO and PO groups decreased the band gap to 2.33, 0.26 and 3.54 eV, respectively. Based on the calculation results, the synergistic effect between CO, N-(C)3, CS and PO groups was discovered, which induced the blue-yellow and blue-green emission wavelength of N/S-CDs and N/P-CDs.

According to the analysis of TEM, XRD, optical properties, chemical structures (FT-IR and XPS) and DFT calculations, we proposed a possible mechanism of X-CDs photoluminescence, as exhibited in Fig. 6 . The PL behaviors of the three CDs are related with the combined action of the eigenstates and surface states. CDs are composed of sp2 conjugated domain and different active functional groups including CO, CO, NH2, N-(C)3, CS, and so on. The multiple energy levels are caused by the single, or co-doping of heteroatoms (N, N/S, and N/P), which could induce the fluorescence emission from various surface states. In this research, the N-CDs have a smaller conjugated system than other CDs, and thus results in stronger fluorescence than that of other CDs and a blue fluorescence emission at the UV light. The abundant CO and a small amount of N-(C)3 can be together conductive to the red-shift of emission wavelength by reducing band gap. For N/S-CDs, the larger conjugated domain and the strong synergistic effect of O-, S-related groups, particularly, CO and CS cause the blue-yellow emission of CDs [40]. In addition, the co-doping of N and P leads to the largest sp2 conjugated core and O-, N- and P-related surface states in N/P-CDs. It is partly responded to the green-blue fluorescence of CDs, and the weak synergy between CO and PO also works for the PL emission of N/P-CDs.

The PL emission spectra of CDs in different pH solutions were analyzed in Fig. 7a-c. With the increment of pH, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increased firstly and then decreased, showing strong fluorescence even in a weak acidic solution. For the three CDs, the optimal fluorescence was obtained at pH = 6. In the high acidic or alkaline solutions, the fluorescence emission of three CDs was also appreciable due to the conjugated effect between multiple surface functional groups and carbon cores [41]. As shown in Fig. 7d, the concentration of KCl displayed insignificant effect on the emission intensity of all the three CDs, which benefited from the steric hindrance effect of surface groups, avoiding the electron transition between core and ions. The results demonstrated their prospect of application in extreme pH and high saline conditions.

Correspondingly, the images of the fluorescent fibers at the UV radiation (405 nm) were observed via CLSM (Fig. S3). After the CDs were loaded into the cotton fibers, the fibers showed a strong fluorescence of in the dark field and superposed field. The CD/fibers had an integrated long morphology and showed the bright and identified fluorescence emission. It is ascribed to the existence of CDs in the nano-scaled pores and lumens of cotton fibers and the great reservation of their fluorescence features. The optical images of the fluorescent cotton fibers and papers were also shown in Fig. S4. It should be noted that the N/S-CDs/fibers became fine due to the chemical cutting effect of N/S dopant (ammonium thiocyanate), which may be not conductive to the adhesion of CDs. Thus, the N/S-CDs/paper was immersed in the solution to ensure the loading of N/S-CDs. The CDs/fibers and their corresponding papers were brown under daylight, which emitted green and blue fluorescence, respectively, under 365 nm excitation because of the differentiated optical properties of CDs. The SEM images and element fractions of the CDs/papers were shown in Fig. S5. This further indicated that the different fluorescence of papers was from the combined CDs controlled by heteroatom-doping.

CDs with excellent optical properties have been a promising candidate as taggants for information protection and anti-counterfeiting [42–45]. To investigate the prospective anti-counterfeiting application of CDs, the CDs were loaded in the PVA medium to prepare the fluorescent films and printable inks. The prepared CDs-PVA films were exhibited in Fig. 8a. The printed alphabets (SCUT) and graphics from the printable inks on different substrates were shown in Fig. 8b-d. As can be seen in Fig. 8ai-iii, all the CDs-PVA films had the high transparency under day light (top) and emitted bright blue and light blue fluorescence at 365 nm excitation. It indicated that the N-CDs/film exhibited the brightest blue-emitted fluorescence, compared to the others, which were consistent with the PL properties. In addition, the SEM images and element fractions of the CDs-PVA films were shown in Fig. S4d-f. It further demonstrated that the great dispersion of CDs in PVA endowed the films with fluorescence features. Fig. 8b-d exhibited the invisible printed characters and graphics under day light and bright or light blue-emitted fluorescent characters and graphics at 365 nm irradiation, which indicated that the obtained invisible fluorescence inks could be widely applicable. In addition, the photostability of the fluorescent paper (N-CDs), film (N/S-CDs) and printable pattern (N/P-CDs) were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 9 , the fluorescent images of the tested models still exhibited bright fluorescence after 2 weeks light bleaching test. Collectively, it is confirmed that the resultant N-CDs, N/S-CDs and N/P-CDs could be used in anti-counterfeiting fibers, films and inks with great information protection performances. Consequently, the obtained CDs from hemicellulose waste can be applied in anti-counterfeiting field for security.

In summary, we successfully synthesized the X-CDs (N-CDs, N/S-CDs and N/P-CDs) via hydrothermal treatment of the separated hemicellulose waste from PHL. The sp2 conjugated structures and surface defect states of CDs were tuned by hydrothermal carbonization and heteroatoms doping (N, N/S, N/P), resulting in the bright blue, deep cyan, and light cyan fluorescence. The XPS spectra and DFT calculations demonstrate that the large size of the sp2 conjugated structure and the synergy effect of CO, N-(C)3, CS, and PO groups promote the reduction of band gaps, and induce the red-shift of PL emission. The grafted abundant surface functional groups (-OH, -NH2) endow CDs with great water solubility, excellent pH and salt stability. Remarkedly, the CDs/fibers emitted green and blue fluorescence due to the in-situ growth of CDs into fibers, and the CDs based fluorescent film and ink exhibit excellent anti-counterfeiting performance, putting new insights into the high-value utilization of hemicellulose waste from papermaking industry.

Qichao Gao: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Xiaohui Guo: Conceptualization, Investigation. Ling Meng: Methodology, Formal analysis. Mengru Liu: Visualization, Software. Li Chen: Validation, Data curation. Hailong Li: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Jian Hu: Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2019B151502043) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22208069).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.126332.
